questions.prompt: Consider the function f(x) = (x + 2)/(x² - 4). Which of the following correctly describes the infinite limits and vertical asymptotes of this function?
options: A) Vertical asymptotes at x = -2 and x = 2; lim(x→2⁺) f(x) = +∞ and lim(x→-2⁻) f(x) = -∞
B) Vertical asymptote only at x = 2; lim(x→2⁺) f(x) = +∞
C) Vertical asymptote only at x = -2; the function has a hole at x = 2
D) No vertical asymptotes; the function is continuous everywhere
questions.correctAnswer: C) Vertical asymptote only at x = -2; the function has a hole at x = 2
questions.explanation: To find vertical asymptotes, we factor the denominator: x² - 4 = (x - 2)(x + 2). The numerator (x + 2) can be canceled with the denominator factor (x + 2), leaving f(x) = 1/(x - 2) for x ≠ -2. This means there is a removable discontinuity (hole) at x = -2, not a vertical asymptote. The vertical asymptote occurs only at x = 2 where the denominator equals zero but the numerator does not. At x = 2, the function exhibits infinite limits: lim(x→2⁺) f(x) = +∞ and lim(x→2⁻) f(x) = -∞.
